title: A Survey of Pun Generation: Datasets, Evaluations and Methodologies
research_questions: What pun datasets have been used or constructed for pun generation research?
How have pun generation methods evolved across conventional methods, classic DNNs, fine-tuned pre-trained language models, prompting, and visual-language models?
What automatic and human evaluation metrics are used to assess pun generation quality?
What challenges and future directions remain for multilingual, multimodal, and prompt-based pun generation?

methods_proposed.name: Survey tree for pun generation
methods_proposed.summary: The paper organizes pun generation research into datasets, methods, and evaluation, with datasets grouped as generic, derived, and human-annotated and methods grouped by technological development timeline.

methods_proposed.name: Five-group methodology taxonomy
methods_proposed.summary: The survey classifies pun generation methods into conventional template/rule systems, classic deep neural networks, fine-tuned pre-trained language models, prompted pre-trained language models, and visual-language models.

humor_theories.detail: The survey summarizes Surprisal as an automatic funniness metric based on local and global surprise around a pun word and its alternative word.
The survey summarizes information-theoretic ambiguity and distinctiveness metrics for linguistic humor in puns.

evaluation_methods.detail: The survey groups automatic metrics into funniness, diversity, and fluency: Ambiguity, Distinctiveness, Surprisal, Unusualness, Dist-1, Dist-2, Perplexity Score, and Structure Succ.

Human evaluation metrics summarized include Success, Funniness, Fluency, Informativeness, Coherence, and Readability; the paper notes use of Likert scales such as 1 (not at all) to 5 (extremely) and binary True/False success judgments.
The survey reports that Chen et al. (2024) used a human A/B test in which annotators compared paired puns generated by their methods and ChatGPT and selected the more humorous pun.
The survey reports that Zeng et al. (2024) replaced human reviewers with GPT-4 to assess readability, funniness, and coherence.

headline_results.finding: The paper collection process initially identified around 150 publications and filtered them to approximately 30 papers focused on pun generation.
Paron is listed as a derived heterographic pun dataset collected from advertisements.
SemEval is described as the first expert-annotated pun dataset and one of the most commonly used datasets in the pun generation community.
The two SemEval enhancements listed are ExPUNations and CUP.
ChinesePun is listed as the first dataset for Chinese homophonic and homographic puns, designed for pun understanding and generation tasks.
The multimodal and multilingual pun datasets listed are Pun Rebus Art and UNPIE.

key_findings: The survey presents pun generation as a creative NLG task that must balance humour, double meaning, coherence, and contextual appropriateness.
The paper categorizes puns into homophonic, heterographic, homographic, and visual puns based on phonetic, graphic, and meaning relationships.
Pun datasets are organized as generic corpora, derived datasets, and human-annotated datasets, with SemEval-2017 Task 7 puns described as especially widely referenced.
Methodological development is described as moving from conventional template and rule systems to classic DNNs, fine-tuned PLMs, prompting, and preliminary visual-language-model work.
The survey argues that English textual pun generation dominates existing research, while multilingual and multimodal pun generation remain underexplored.

The paper emphasizes that both automatic and human evaluation have important weaknesses: automatic metrics miss semantic or logical quality, while human judgments are subjective and culturally dependent.
limitations: Some relevant works may have been missed due to variations in search keywords.
The survey’s exploration of other pun categories is limited, including recursive puns and antanaclasis.
The study does not cover the entire historical scope nor the latest advancements following the survey because the field is rapidly developing.
Surprisal-based automatic evaluation is context-dependent and local Surprisal is highly sensitive to the choice of local window size.
Dist-1 and Dist-2 fail to capture semantic diversity, and Perplexity Score does not necessarily imply semantic coherence or logical consistency.
Human evaluation is subjective, especially for readability and funniness, and many studies do not provide detailed evaluator background information.
